coredata.dc:title: Rational synthesis of micronano dendritic ZVI@Fe3O4 modified with carbon quantum dots and oxygen vacancies for accelerating Fenton-like oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Xia, Qixing
Yao, Zhongping
Zhang, Dongjie
Li, Dongqi
Zhang, Zhirong
Jiang, Zhaohua
coredata.dc:description: The zero valence iron (ZVI) Fenton-like catalyst has two main defects: one defect is that the surface passivation impairs the reusability of catalysts, and the other defect is the deactivation under neutral working pH. To overcome these problems, we synthesized a dendrite-like ZVI@Fe3O4 composite modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and oxygen vacancies (OVs) by electrodeposition and solvothermal treatments. The results showed that the CQDs (approximately 5 nm) with polar C-groups on the surface of the composites were derived from the carbonization of glucose during thermal treatment, and the OVs (atomic scale) at the junction of Fe3O4 and the CQDs were induced by the reduction of the CQDs. The synthesized composite possessed excellent catalytic activity and reusability. Under the condition of 6 mmol/L H2O2, the synthesized composite catalyst with the dose of 0.10 g/L completely removed 35 ppm phenol within 10 min at pH 4.0 and within 60 min at neutral pH. Furthermore, the catalytic mechanism was proposed. The reducing effect of CQDs accelerated the Fe3+/Fe2+ cycle, and the surface polar C-groups promoted the adsorption of phenol molecules; the OVs induced the formation of acidic micro regions and facilitated the dissociation of the OO bonds in H2O2 into OH, extending the application range of pH from ~4.0 to the neutral condition during the Fenton-like process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Micronano dendritic ZVI@Fe3O4 catalyst
Carbon quantum dots
Oxygen vacancies
Fenton-like oxidation
Phenol degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Modification of dendritic ZVI@Fe3O4 with CQDs and OVs
Heterogeneous Fenton-like oxidation of phenol degradation
Effect of degradation parameters on E160/H2O2 system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images, HRTEM images and schematic structure of ZVI (a, d, g), E160 (b, e, h) and EG 160 (c, f, i).
Raman (a) and XRD (b) patterns of E160 and EG160, the comparisons of XPS O1s spectra of E160 and EG160: 0 nm (c) and 5 nm (d) of depth.
XPS C1s spectra of ZVI (a), E160 (b) and EG160 (c), and the zeta potential (ζ) measured at pH 4.0 (d).
Schematic diagrams of surface evolution on EG160 over time (a) and the formation mechanism of OVs (b).

The phenol gradation via single H2O2, ZVI, E160 and EG160 without or with adding H2O2 at pH 4.0 (a), the phenol gradation via ZVI, E160 and EG160 with adding H2O2 at neutral pH (b), and the corresponding Fe leaching (c), EPR spectra of Fenton-like process with different activators (d). (Other experimental conditions: 35 ppm phenol, 0.10 g/L catalyst amount and 6 mmol/L H2O2 content.)
Effect of catalyst dose (a), H2O2 content (b) and pH value (c) on phenol degradation experiments via EG160/H2O2 system, and schematic diagram of OVs inducing Brønsted acid micro-sites (d).
Comparison of Recycling experiments of ZVI (a), E160 (b) and EG160 (c) reused for phenol degradation, and the schematic diagram of CQDs assisting Fenton-like process.

Immobilization of EG160 by magnetic attraction (a) and the simulating natural Fenton-like oxidation of phenol by immobilized EG160/H2O2 system (b). (Other conditions: 0.20 g/L EG160, immobilized with about 23 cm2 by magnetic attraction, without pH pre-adjustment or stirring.)
Schematic diagrams of Enhanced Fenton-like process via EG160/H2O2 system.
The comparison of the Fenton-like performance for phenol removal by EG160 and other catalysts.

row.entry: ZVI with N-containing activated carbon (Messele et al., 2014)
Fe3O4/SiO2 with sulfate coating (H. Wang et al., 2017)
Fe3O4/montmorillonite (H. Jin et al., 2017; M. Jin et al., 2017)

head.ce:title: Rational synthesis of micronano dendritic ZVI@Fe3O4 modified with carbon quantum dots and oxygen vacancies for accelerating Fenton-like oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The zero valence iron (ZVI) Fenton-like catalyst has two main defects: one defect is that the surface passivation impairs the reusability of catalysts, and the other defect is the deactivation under neutral working pH. To overcome these problems, we synthesized a dendrite-like ZVI@Fe3O4 composite modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and oxygen vacancies (OVs) by electrodeposition and solvothermal treatments. The results showed that the CQDs (approximately 5 nm) with polar C-groups on the surface of the composites were derived from the carbonization of glucose during thermal treatment, and the OVs (atomic scale) at the junction of Fe3O4 and the CQDs were induced by the reduction of the CQDs. The synthesized composite possessed excellent catalytic activity and reusability. Under the condition of 6 mmol/L H2O2, the synthesized composite catalyst with the dose of 0.10 g/L completely removed 35 ppm phenol within 10 min at pH 4.0 and within 60 min at neutral pH. Furthermore, the catalytic mechanism was proposed. The reducing effect of CQDs accelerated the Fe3+/Fe2+ cycle, and the surface polar C-groups promoted the adsorption of phenol molecules; the OVs induced the formation of acidic micro regions and facilitated the dissociation of the OO bonds in H2O2 into OH, extending the application range of pH from ~4.0 to the neutral condition during the Fenton-like process.

ce:section.ce:para: Phenol released in the environment from effluents of refineries and petrochemical and pharmaceutical industries is one of the most important refractory organics and can result in serious damage to human beings and other life forms (Zazo et al., 2005). Fenton and Fenton-like reactions are considered promising approaches for degrading organic pollutants due to the generated hydroxyl radicals (OH) with high standard oxidation potential (2.8 V) (Qin et al., 2015; Xu and Wang, 2012). As an effective Fenton-like catalyst, zero valance iron (ZVI) has drawn the attention of many scientists in the field of water purification because of its capacity to activate H2O2 into OH and its easy magnetic separation (Bremner et al., 2006; Fu et al., 2014; He et al., 2016; Lu et al., 2007; Ma et al., 2016; Xiao et al., 2015). However, the surface passivation of ZVI leads to the poor stability in air and aqueous environments and poor reusability, thus restricting its application (Harada et al., 2016; J. Liu et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2013). To solve this problem, many labs have encapsulated ZVI with various carbon materials such as graphite oxides, carbon nanotubes and carbon nitrides (Fan et al., 2010; Hussain et al., 2016; Li et al., 2015; Yao et al., 2016) through reducing high-valence Fe into Fe2+ to suppress the formation of the passivation layer on ZVI (Li et al., 2015; C. Liu et al., 2014; Pradhan et al., 2013). However, the carbon resources are usually high cost, and the synthesis processes are not always green, i.e., processes involving strong-acid processing or high temperature treatment. Therefore, the search for alternative suitable materials has led to an increased interest in carbon-coated iron-based Fenton-like catalysts.

A challenge for single ZVI catalyst in Fenton-like processes is the narrow working pH range. Generally, the suitable pH for activating H2O2 into OH is pH 3.0 (Bremner et al., 2006; Donadelli et al., 2018), and, therefore, the pH value in the degradation system always needs to be adjusted. At present, there are two strategies to enhance the Fenton-like process at high pH values. One strategy is to provide extra energy to enhance the Fenton-like process via electricity (Shi et al., 2015; Wei et al., 2015), ultrasounds (Savun-Hekimoğlu and Ince, 2017) or UV–Vis radiation (Dias et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2014), but these methods increase the complexity and the high cost of the degradation systems. Another strategy is to design novel Fenton-like catalysts through surface modification that can run well at near neutral conditions. Sulfur-modification of iron-based catalysts (Du et al., 2016; Y. Liu et al., 2016; J. Wang et al., 2017a) can extend the working pH range from 3.0 to 7.0 by providing an acid environment to release more Fe2+ into homogeneous systems for activating H2O2 into OH. The modification by oxygen vacancies (OVs) (H. Jin et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017; H. Wang et al., 2017) has become another promising method for changing the surface states of catalysts. OVs possess a large amount of localized electrons that endow the oxygen-deficient surfaces with an electron-rich characteristic for stretching small molecules, such as H2O2 (Jiang et al., 2012; Li et al., 2017; Moya et al., 2015; Nair et al., 2017). Such a stretching effect makes the single O − O bond in H2O2 around the OVs weaker than that in free H2O2 (J. Wang et al., 2017a; J. Wang et al., 2017b). In this way, the Fenton-like process is not sensitive to pH, and therefore the working pH range is expanded.

In our previous work, we prepared dendritic ZVI with good dispersibility and a large specific surface area through electrodeposition (Xia et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2013). To further improve the surface passivation and expand the working pH range, we creatively designed a multiscale composite Fenton-like catalyst containing micronano dendritic ZVI@Fe3O4 core-shell, nanoscale CQDs and atomic-scale OVs, with glucose selected as a green carbon-source during the solvothermal treatment. The composite Fenton-like catalyst presented an ultracatalytic capacity and excellent reusability for the removal of phenol, and the catalytic mechanisms for accelerating Fenton-like reaction are discussed. This work not only provides novel solutions for the green design of new composite catalysts but also presents a promising approach in the field of water purification.

The hierarchical, dendritic micronano structure ZVI was prepared first by electrodeposition. Briefly, the electrolyte solution was composed of 0.5 mol/L FeSO4 and 50 mL/L anhydrous ethanol. The current density was 1.6 A/dm2, and the electrodeposited time was 20 s. After electrodeposition, the product was collected in a centrifuge tube and washed with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol three times. The product was dried at 60 °C under vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of dendritic ZVI@Fe3O4 with CQDs and OVs
ce:section.ce:para: The solvothermal solvent was composed of 95 % ethanol, 5 % water and 8.0 g/L glucose. The dendritic ZVI was placed in the Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and treated at 100 °C, 130 °C or 160 °C for 24 h, and the obtained samples were labeled as EG100, EG130 and EG160, respectively. After treatment, the samples were collected by centrifugation and washed with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol three times. For comparison, the ZVI was also treated in the same solvothermal solution without glucose at 160 °C for 24 h and labeled as E160.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were recorded on an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku D/max-γB diffractometer, Japan, Cu Kα, λ = 0.15406 nm). The morphology and microstructure of the sample were characterized with field-emission scanning electron (NanoLab 600i SEM, America) and high-resolution transmission electron (FEI Tecnai G2 F30, America) microscopes. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted with a PHI 5400 ESCA system (America, Mg Kα radiation at 1253.6 eV). Before XPS detection, the surface of samples was etched with an argon ion laser at 4000 V with 0.5 μA at 0 nm, 5 nm and 10 nm in depth. The removal rate was approximately 1 nm/min. To evaluate the acidic properties of the samples, the temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia (NH3-TPD) was carried out (Finetec FINESORB-3010 instrument, China). BET surface areas (S BET) were calculated from nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms determined at 77 K using a 3H-2000PS1 surface analyzer (the sample was outgassed under vacuum at 473 K).

The degradation experiments of phenol were carried out at 303 K, and the original phenol solution (phenol concentration: 35 mg/L) was 50 mL. Prior to the degradation, the pH of the solution was adjusted with 0.1 mol/L H2SO4. The catalyst sample was added to the solution and dispersed by ultrasound. The degradation process was initiated once a given amount of H2O2 was added to the phenol solution under the vigorous mechanical stirring. During degradation, a desired amount of solution without catalyst powder was withdrawn with a pipette at given time intervals, and the phenol concentration was measured with 4-aminoantipyrine at 510 nm on a UV/Vis spectrophotometer (Lambda XLS, PerkinElmer, America). The concentrations of the ferrous ions and total irons leaching from the catalysts were evaluated by the 1, 10-phenanothroline method.

Fig. 1 shows the SEM and HRTEM images and the schematic structure of ZVI, E160 and EG160. After the solvothermal treatment, both E160 and EG160 showed a typical micronano dendritic structure with a length of approximately 10 μm and a width of approximately 3 μm (Fig. 1a–c). The BET surface areas of ZVI, E160 and EG160 were 41 m2/g, 36 m2/g and 34 m2/g (Fig. S1), approximate to those of other nano-ZVI materials (Choe et al., 2000; Sun et al., 2006; T. Liu et al., 2014; Xiao et al., 2015) in Table S1. The EDS results (Table S2) indicated that E160 and EG160 were oxidized and deposited with C, and the amount of C deposited on EG160 was more than that on E160. Additionally, HRTEM images of E160 and EG160 proved the formation of a considerable amount of Fe3O4. The HRTEM image of EG160 shows the striations spacing with 0.203 nm, 0.252 nm, 0.296 nm and 0.504 nm, corresponding to Fe (110), Fe3O4 (311), Fe3O4 (220) and CQDs (220), respectively; the striation corresponding to the CQDs were not observed on the HRTEM image of E160. Therefore, it can be inferred that the deposited C on E160 was from ethanol, whereas that on EG160 comes from both ethanol and glucose. The generation of CQDs in the sample EG160 was mainly attributed to the thermal decomposition of glucose during the solvothermal process according to reaction (1)
(1)
C 6
H 12
O 6
→ 6C
CQDs
+
H 2
O

To further confirm the formation of CQDs, we dissolved ZVI, E160 and EG160 in dilute sulfuric acid, and the obtained solutions were measured by UV–Vis adsorption spectrum. Fig. S2a only presents the peak of 256 nm in the EG160-contained solution, which corresponded to CQDs (Gao et al., 2015). CQDs were not only deposited on EG160 but also entered into the solvothermal solution, which was verified by TEM images of the solvothermal solution of EG160 (Fig. S2b). Fig. 1g–i depicts the schematic structures of ZVI, E160 and EG160. All these samples had a dendritic structure with micron-sized trunks and nanosized branches. There are clear boundaries between the striations of Fe (110) of 0.203 nm and Fe3O4 (311) of 0.252 nm in the HRTEM images of E160 (Fig. 1e). However, the striations of Fe and Fe3O4 in the HRTEM images of EG160 were interlaced (Figs. 1f and S3), which may suggest that some Fe3O4 molecules were reduced to Feo in the reducing environment provided by the generated CQDs (Lu et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2013).

Fig. S4 presents the phase composition of the samples under different solvothermal temperatures. With the increase in the solvothermal temperature, the amount of CQDs gradually increased (Fig. S4a) while the amount of Fe3O4 in the samples decreased before increasing (Fig. S4b). This result suggested that the increase in the temperature facilitated the formation of CQDs not only on the samples but also in the solution (Fig. S4c). The increase in the temperature had two opposite effects: one effect was positive and increased Fe3O4 and CQDs; the other effect was the increase in CQDs to the detriment of the oxidation of Fe due to the enhancement of the reducing effect. The changing trend of Fe3O4 (Fig. S4b) could be attributed to the competing results of the two aspects, namely, the reducing effect was stronger than the oxidation of Fe at a low temperature (130 °C) while the oxidation of Fe was stronger than the reducing effect at a high temperature (160 °C).

As a result of the strong reduction effects of CQDs, low-valance Fe (0 and +2) was easily generated in the Fe3O4 shell near the CQDs layer. Subsequently, the self-replacement of high-valence Fe (+3) by low-valence Fe (0 and +2) produced OVs on the Fe3O4 framework similar to OVs generated in metal oxides modified by low-valence atoms (H. Jin et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2015; Nair et al., 2017) or treated in the hypoxia surroundings (Li et al., 2017). The generation of OVs on the Fe3O4 framework occurred by reaction (2).
(2)
m C + FeO ·
Fe 2
O 3
→ m C − O +
Fe
1 + 2 m
O ·
Fe
2 − 2 m
O
3 − m
+ m
OVs

Generally, the formation of OVs was related to the nonstoichiometry Fe3O4 framework, which could be further verified by Raman, XRD, and XPS analyses, as shown in Fig. 2 . The Fe-O Raman peak for EG160 (Fig. 2a) shifted positively, which inferred that there existed defects and lattice disorders in the Fe3O4 framework because of the sensitivity of Raman scattering (H. Jin et al., 2017). In the XRD analysis (Fig. 2b), the decrease in 2θ of Fe3O4 (311) illustrated the increase in the interplanar spacing, which corresponded to the formation of OVs (Fu et al., 2015). The missing of partial O atoms on the Fe3O4 framework weakened the force of attraction between Fe and O, thus increasing the interplanar spacing from 0.252 to 0.253.

O1s XPS analysis also demonstrated the formation of OVs (Fig. 2c and d). The O1s spectra of the catalyst surfaces (0 nm, Fig. 2c) were almost identical and corresponded to C-O and Fe-O. However, when the catalyst was etched approximately 5 nm deep by electron sputtering, the O1s spectra of the exposed surface of EG160 shifted to a more negative binding energy than that of E160 (5 nm, Fig. 2d). Lower binding energy of O1s indicated the nonstoichiometric oxygen atoms within the catalyst and the generation of OVs (Li et al., 2017). Moreover, the changes of O1s XPS with sputtering depth revealed that the formed OVs existed on the Fe3O4 layer near the CQDs layer and not directly on the surface, i.e., the OVs were located at the junction of Fe3O4 and CQDs, which was beneficial for the stability of OVs (Feng et al., 2018).

Fig. 3 shows the XPS C1s spectra and zeta potential of ZVI, E160 and EG160. The peak of 284.6 eV observed on the C1s spectra of ZVI corresponds to carbon contaminate. For E160 and EG160, the C1s peaks could be deconvoluted into three peaks: sp3C at 284.6 eV, C-OH at 285.6 eV (Hussain et al., 2016) and COOH at 288.3 eV (Luo et al., 2015); these groups derive from the thermal decomposition of ethanol and glucose. After the addition of glucose, EG160 had more polar C-groups than E160. As previously shown, many polar C-groups accompany CQDs and amorphous carbon, which change the charge characteristics on the catalyst surface (Feng et al., 2012). In Fig. 3d, the zeta potential (ζ) of ZVI, E160 and EG160 at pH 4.0 were +8.8 mV, −11.2 mV and −15.6 mV, respectively. The conversion of ζ from positive to negative matched well with the deposition of polar C-groups. EG160 possessed the more negative potential than the other samples due to the increase of the number of polar C-groups.

Based on these results, we described the process of the formation of CQDs and OVs on EG160, as displayed in Fig. 4 . First, the Fe3O4 framework formed because of the oxidation of ZVI. Second, CQDs with polar C-groups were generated and deposited on the Fe3O4 framework. Third, the reduction effect of CQDs led to the formation of low-valence Fe (0 and +2) that replaced the original high-valence Fe (+3) in the Fe3O4 framework (reaction (3)) so that OVs were generated to maintain the charge balance in Fe3O4 framework (Fig. 4b). The formation of OVs was due to the reaction of CQDs and Fe3O4, and thus OVs were mainly distributed around CQDs-Fe3O4 regions and not ZVI-Fe3O4 regions. Moreover, the amount of CQDs was related to the solvothermal temperature, indicating that it could be controlled by changing the solvothermal conditions (Fig. S5 and Tables S3–S7). Hence, the multiscale design of EG160 was fulfilled through electrodeposition and solvothermal treatment: dendritic structure (micronano scale), CQDs (nano scale) and OVs (atomic scale).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heterogeneous Fenton-like oxidation of phenol degradation

ce:section.ce:para: The Fenton-like catalytic activity of catalysts was studied by phenol degradation at pH 4.0 (Fig. 5a). Nearly no phenol was degraded in the solution with only H2O2, revealing that individual H2O2 was not effective for phenol degradation. In the solution with only catalyst, the decrease in phenol content was caused by the adsorption by the catalyst, and the order of the adsorption capacities was EG160 > E160 > ZVI, which also matched the zeta potentials of three composite catalysts. Thus, the negative charge was beneficial for attracting the phenol molecules with phenolic hydroxyl group.

In the catalyst/H2O2 system, phenol was effectively removed by three kinds of catalysts, and the order of the catalytic capacity was EG160 > E160 > ZVI, similar to the order of the adsorption capacity, indicating that the adsorption effect could shorten the required migration distance of phenol towards active sites on the catalyst and play a positive role in phenol degradation (Li et al., 2018). Extraordinarily, 35 ppm phenol was almost entirely degraded (99%) within 10 min in EG160/H2O2 system. However, the dose of the catalyst was only 0.1 g/L, much lower than in previous reports (M. Jin et al., 2017; Liao et al., 2009; Y. Wang et al., 2017).

Fig. 5b displays the catalytic capacity of the catalysts in the degradation system at neutral condition (pH 6.0). Neither ZVI nor E160 was able to activate H2O2, and no more phenol was degraded. By contrast, EG160/H2O2 removed 99% phenol within 60 min. Fig. 5c shows the corresponding Fe leaching in different Fenton-like systems under 60 min. Fe leaching in all systems was lower than 2 ppm, which is significant for avoiding the secondary pollution from iron ions during Fenton-like processes. Moreover, the Fe leaching at neutral conditions was much lower than at pH 4.0, suggesting that the homogeneous process was greatly inhibited in the neutral condition. Due to a larger amount of CQDs deposited on the EG160, the Fe leaching amount of EG160 is restrained and lower than those of ZVI and E160 at pH 4.0.

Fig. 5d is the electron-paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra of the different Fenton-like systems at pH 4.0, using DMPO as the spin-trapping agent. All three systems have the fourfold characteristic peaks in the EPR spectra with ratio of 1:2:2:1, confirming that the hydroxyl radicals (OH) were the primary reactive oxidizing species. Additionally, the order of the peak intensity was EG160 ≈ E160 > ZVI. This result was because amorphous carbon or CQDs could reduce Fe3+ into Fe2+, which facilitated the transformation of H2O2 into OH. Therefore, the amount of OH generated in EG160/H2O2 and E160/H2O2 was more than that in ZVI/H2O2.

In addition, Fig. S6 displays the homogeneous Fenton processes with various Fe2+ concentrations, based on the results in Fig. 5c. With an increase in Fe2+ concentration, the rate of phenol degradation was obviously improved. Compared with Fig. 5a, EG160 presented better catalytic activity than the homogeneous Fe2+ (the Fe2+ concentration was the same as the Fe leaching amount of the EG160/H2O2 system), which indicated that the heterogeneous process played the dominate role in the degradation reaction. Fig. S7 presents the effects of radical scavengers (KI/CH3OH) on phenol degradation in the EG160/H2O2 system. The degradation efficiency in the Fenton-like systems with KI or CH3OH decreased significantly, further indicating that the Fenton-like reaction in the EG160/H2O2 system was indeed based on heterogeneous (catalyst surface) processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of degradation parameters on E160/H2O2 system
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6a shows the effect of EG160 dose on phenol degradation at pH 4.0. With increasing EG160 dose, the degradation rate of phenol was enhanced. It was clearly noted that more EG160 activated more H2O2 into OH for the degradation of phenol. Considering the economic cost, the optimal dose of EG160 is 0.1 g/L.

H2O2 also played a crucial role in phenol degradation, and the effect of H2O2 content on EG160/H2O2 is shown in Fig. 6b. When H2O2 content was increased, the degradation efficiency of phenol first increased and then decreased, and the optimal H2O2 content was 6 mmol/L. This finding is because the appropriate improvement in H2O2 could promote the generation of OH, but excessive H2O2 suppressed the phenol degradation by trapping the OH (the standard redox potential of O2H is 0.9 V, lower than that of OH (2.7 V)), according to reaction (3).
(3)
H 2
O 2
+ ˙ OH →
H 2
O + ˙
O 2
H

As the H2O2 content was >6 mmol/L, an obvious initial stage with relatively low degradation rate was observed in the experiments. Over the time of the degradation process, the degradation rate accelerated when the excessive H2O2 content declined into a suitable range. However, when excessive H2O2 was used in Fenton-like systems with ZVI or E160 as the activator, the degradation rate in the two systems could not be recovered, and the entire degradation process was restrained (Fig. S8).

pH value was another important factor in the Fenton-like process. The influences of pH value on EG160/H2O2 were investigated (Fig. 6c), and the degradation rate of phenol appeared dependent on the pH value. With increases in pH, the phenol degradation declined gradually, and an apparent slow stage (usually considered as inducing stage) appeared in the degradation curves. Once the phenol started to be oxidized, the degradation products entered into the solution and lowered the pH (Zazo et al., 2005). Thus, the rate of degradation reaction was accelerated subsequently.

The kinetic curves of phenol gradation under different degradation parameters are shown in Fig. S9. The kinetic constants increased with the increase in catalyst dose, or the decrease in H2O2 content and pH value. Under high H2O2 concentration (>6 mmol/L) or high pH (>5.0), the degradation processes could be divided into two stages: the first slow stage and the second rapid stage. Based on reaction (3), the excessive H2O2 would react with OH into O2H, which played a negative role on the oxidation of phenol, so that the kinetic constants were small at the first stage under high H2O2 concentration. As the H2O2 content decreased into a suitable range over the time of the degradation process, the consequent kinetic constant increased at the second stage. The high pH value of system would lower the generation rate of OH, resulting in a small kinetic constant in the first stage, as like reaction (4). As the degradation process went on, the degradation products' such as small molecule acids entered the solution (Zazo et al., 2005), which decreased the pH value accordingly. Thus, the kinetic constant of phenol degradation became large at the second stage.
(4)
☰
Fe
2 +
+
H +
+
H 2
O 2
→ ☰
Fe
3 +
+
H 2
O + ˙ OH

These results reveal that EG160 had a strong catalytic activity even at excessive H2O2 and the neutral condition, which could be associated with the assist-activating effect of OVs on the H2O2 molecule, similar to previous reports (H. Jin et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2015; Nair et al., 2017). OVs could stretch the OO bond in H2O2 molecules and reduce the activation energy of H2O2 into OH, which helped EG160 overcome the adverse impacts of excessive H2O2 and high system pH. Conversely, E160 and ZVI presented non-activation under the same conditions.

However, the interactions of H2O and OVs cannot be neglected in aqueous solution. Herein, we provide a new assumption to clarify an alternative influence mechanism of OVs on working pH, as shown in Fig. 6d. OVs hold strong attraction to the oxygen atoms in H2O molecular and grasp the localized electrons cloud around H2O, which make the hydrogen atoms more extremely exposed. Therefore, the H2O molecules around OVs have the ability to supply protons, which could be considered as acid micro-regions (reaction (5)).
(5)
OVs +
H 2
O → OVs −
O
2 −
+
2H +

To support this assumption, the TPD-NH3 was measured, and the results are shown in Fig. S10. Compared with E160, there were two peaks of NH3-adsorption on EG160, indicating that EG160 possessed a certain acid property owing to the presence of OVs. Therefore, the induced acid micro-regions could effectively help EG160 to activate H2O2 into OH.

In water treatment, the reusability of the catalyst is important for meeting the requirements of saving cost and avoiding secondary pollution. The reusability of the catalysts was investigated by cycle experiments, as presented in Fig. 7 a–c. The reused ZVI was passivated rapidly, and the phenol degradation efficiency during Run 4 was only 22% within 60 min. E160 could be employed to remove almost 90% of the phenol during Runs 1–4 within 40 min, though the degradation rate gradually declined each time. In the EG160/H2O2 system, 99% of the phenol was completely removed during Run 4 within 20 min, suggesting that EG160 possessed more effective reusability than ZVI and E160.

Fig. 7d illustrates the effects of CQDs on the Fenton-like reaction. First, CQDs of EG160 served as a reductant for enhancing the recycle of Fe3+ and Fe2+ (reaction (6)), and the effective iron species (Fe2+) on catalyst surfaces were conserved to result in good EG160 reusability, similar to other carbon materials in other reports (Li et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015; Xia et al., 2018; Zubir et al., 2015). Second, many polar C-groups on the CQDs changed the zeta potential, which was more helpful for attracting phenol molecules around catalyst.
(6)
☰
Fe
3 +
+ C +
H 2
O → ☰
Fe
2 +
+ C ▬ OH +
H +

Because particle catalysts are difficult to recollect in water environments, we employed a simple magnetic-attraction device to immobilize 0.20 g/L EG160 on the external surface of a PTFE flat (apparent area of 23 cm2) to conduct the Fenton-like process without pH pre-adjustment and stir ring, as shown in Fig. 8a. After degradation, the PTFE flat loaded with the catalyst was removed and simply flushed with deionized water before reuse during the next run. >95% of the phenol could be effectively removed within 180 min in three runs, though the degradation rate decreased slightly for each run (Fig. 8b). Thus, under the immobilization conditions, EG160 still possessed a promising catalytic activity in the neutral condition.

From the above analysis, the unique characteristics EG160, such as micronano dendritic morphology, ZVI@Fe3O4 core-shell structure, CQD modification (nano scale) and OVs depth distribution (atomic scale), contributed to the ultracatalytic properties of the Fenton-like degradation of phenol, as illustrated in Fig. 9 . Micronano dendritic structures provided a large surface reaction area and avoided the agglomeration of the catalyst, and the ZVI@Fe3O4 core-shell had a strong redox potential for activating H2O2 (reactions (4) and (7)) and made the catalyst easily recoverable by magnetism separation.
(7)
☰
Fe o
+ ☰
Fe
3 +
→ 2 ☰
Fe
2 +

Importantly, CQDs could accelerate the Fe3+/Fe2+ cycle due to their strong reduction effect (reaction (6)) and attracted phenol molecules because of the associated polar C-groups on the surface, improving the OH yield and shortening the required migration distance of phenol to active sites, as presented in Fig. 9a. OVs not only lowered the activation energy of H2O2 dissociation into OH by stretching the OO bonds but also provided acid micro-regions supporting the extension of the application pH range (reaction (5)), as shown in Fig. 9b ①–②. Moreover, the amounts of CQDs and OVs could be improved by increasing the solvothermal temperature, which could endow the catalysts obtained at higher temperature with better catalytic activities (EG160 > EG130 > EG100), as shown in Fig. S11.

Table 1 shows the comparison of Fenton-like catalysts in this work and other reports (M. Jin et al., 2017; Liao et al., 2009; Messele et al., 2014; Minella et al., 2014; Pradhan et al., 2013; Segura et al., 2012; J. Wang et al., 2017a; Y. Wang et al., 2017). Considering the low dose of the as-prepared catalyst, small metal ion leaching, good stability, large range of working pH and no-extra apparatus, EG160 presented the most competitive catalytic capacity among various Fenton-like catalysts.

In summary, we rationally synthesized micronano dendritic ZVI@Fe3O4 with CQDs and OVs via green agents and facile preparation methods. The obtained catalyst (EG160) had much better catalytic activity, broader application pH range and lower Fe leaching in the Fenton-like process. The formation of CQDs was mainly due to the carbonization of glucose during the solvothermal treatment, and their strong reducing effect further led to the generation of OVs through the reduction of Fe3O4.

The synergetic effects of the structure morphology and the multicomponents of the Fenton-like catalysts provided the ultracatalytic activity for the degradation of phenol. Typical dendritic structures contributed good dispersibility and high specific surface area. CQDs promoted the transformation of Fe3+/Fe2+ and the adsorption of phenol molecules on the surface of catalyst, which enhanced OH yields and shortened the migration distance of phenol to the active sites. OVs facilitated the dissociation of H2O2 into OH by stretching the OO bonds and provided acid micro-regions to extend the working pH range.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51571076) and Open project of State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment of Harbin Institute of Technology (No. HCK201716).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2019.03.435.
